title: Softmax: Turning Scores into Probabilities
concept: Softmax is used in the final output layer for multi-class problems: it converts a whole list of raw scores into probabilities that sum to 100%, spotlighting the most likely category.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what makes ReLU so fast compared to Sigmoid or Tanh?

options.text: Its rule is dead simple — chop negatives to 0, pass positives through unchanged
It squashes numbers into a 0-to-1 range
It only works on the output layer

exercises.explain: Right — ReLU's simplicity is exactly what makes it fast. Now: what happens when a neuron has to choose between MANY categories, not just yes/no?

cards.title: Picking a winner among many options
cards.body: Sigmoid decides yes-or-no. Tanh leans toward one of two opposite directions. But what if a network has to choose between ten possible categories — cat, dog, bird, fish, and so on? None of the earlier functions were built for that job.

cards.title: Softmax: scores become percentages
cards.body: Softmax takes a whole list of raw output scores — one per category — and converts them into probabilities that all add up to exactly 100% (or 1.0). The category with the highest raw score ends up with the highest probability.

cards.title: Splitting a vote
cards.body: Think of Softmax like counting votes and reporting results as percentages: every candidate gets a share, and all the shares must add up to exactly 100%. Softmax does the same thing for a network's raw scores — redistributing total confidence across every possible category.

cards.title: Classifying a photo into categories
cards.body: Raw scores from a photo classifier: Cat 5, Dog 2, Bird 1. After Softmax: Cat ≈90%, Dog ≈7%, Bird ≈3% — all three add up to 100%, and the highest raw score (Cat) becomes the winning pick.

cards.title: Not a substitute for Sigmoid
cards.body: You might think you could just use Sigmoid instead of Softmax to choose between many categories. But Sigmoid scores one independent 0-to-1 decision at a time. Softmax is built so ALL categories' probabilities are linked together and forced to add up to 100% — it's the output-layer tool for multi-class problems, while Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU typically live in hidden layers.

cards.title: One decision vs. many linked decisions

left.text:
One neuron, one 0–1 score
Used for a single yes/no decision

right.text:
A whole layer of scores
All add up to 100%, picks a category

exercises.prompt: What is special about the probabilities that Softmax produces for a list of categories?

options.text: They all add up to exactly 100% (1.0)
Each one is independently between -1 and 1
Only the highest score gets a probability

exercises.explain: Correct! Softmax's whole set of output probabilities always sums to exactly 100%.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Softmax is typically used in a hidden layer rather than the output layer.
exercises.explain: False. Softmax is the classic choice for the OUTPUT layer of a multi-class network.

exercises.sentence: Softmax converts a list of raw ___ into probabilities that sum to 100%.
exercises.explain: Softmax takes raw output scores, one per category, and turns the whole list into percentages.

exercises.prompt: A network needs to classify a photo into exactly one of 5 animal categories. Which activation function belongs in its final output layer?
exercises.explain: Softmax is built specifically for picking one winner among several linked categories in the output layer.

exercises.prompt: You're designing a network that classifies handwritten digits into one of 10 categories (0 through 9). Which activation function belongs in the final output layer to turn raw scores into a clean set of probabilities across all 10 digits, and why?

options.text: Softmax, because it converts a whole list of raw scores into probabilities that sum to 100%, spotlighting the most likely digit
ReLU, because it's the fastest activation function available
Sigmoid, because it works for any number of categories
Tanh, because negative scores need to be allowed

exercises.explain: Exactly right! Softmax turns 10 raw scores into 10 probabilities that add up to 100%, spotlighting the most likely digit. You now know how a neuron decides what to output at every stage of a network — input to hidden to output, weighted, activated, and squashed into a final answer. Next up: how a network actually learns the right weights in the first place.
